questions.question: The heights (in inches) of 11 basketball players: 72, 74, 75, 76, 78, 79, 80, 81, 83, 85, 88. What is the median, and what is the IQR?
questions.explanation: Median is the 6th value: 79. Q1 = 75, Q3 = 83. IQR = 83 - 75 = 8.

questions.question: The mean of 6 numbers is 18. When a seventh number is added, the mean becomes 20. What is the seventh number?
questions.explanation: Sum of the 6 numbers = 6 × 18 = 108. Let $x$ be the seventh number. $(108 + x)/7 = 20 \rightarrow 108 + x = 140 \rightarrow x = 32$.

questions.question: Two classes took a test. Class A: mean = 78, standard deviation = 5. Class B: mean = 78, standard deviation = 12. Which statement is true?
options: Class A's scores are more spread out than Class B's
Class B's scores are more spread out than Class A's
Both classes have the same spread
Class A has a higher median than Class B
questions.explanation: A larger standard deviation means more variability. Class B's scores vary more widely around the same mean of 78.

questions.question: A box plot for test scores shows: min = 42, Q1 = 58, median = 72, Q3 = 85, max = 98. Using the 1.5 × IQR rule, are there any outliers?
questions.explanation: IQR = 85 - 58 = 27. Lower fence = 58 - 40.5 = 17.5. Upper fence = 85 + 40.5 = 125.5. Both 42 and 98 fall within the fences.

questions.question: A scatter plot shows hours of sleep (x) vs. test score (y). The line of best fit is $y = 3.2x + 48$ with correlation coefficient $r = 0.85$. What does $r$ tell you?
options: There is a strong negative correlation
There is a strong positive correlation
There is a weak positive correlation
questions.explanation: $r = 0.85$ is close to 1, indicating a strong positive correlation. More sleep is associated with higher test scores.

questions.question: Using the line of best fit $y = 3.2x + 48$, where $x$ is hours of sleep and $y$ is test score, what is the predicted score for 7 hours of sleep?

The table shows movie preferences by gender. What is the joint relative frequency of a movie being an Action movie preferred by a man? Express as a percent.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Comedy} & \text{Action} & \text{Drama} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Men} & 28 & 42 & 10 & 80 \\ \hline \text{Women} & 32 & 18 & 30 & 80 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 60 & 60 & 40 & 160 \\ \hline\end{array}$$
questions.explanation: Men who prefer Action = 42. Grand total = 160. Joint relative frequency = 42/160 = 0.2625 = 26.25%.

questions.question:
Using the same movie preferences table, given that a movie is a Drama, what is the conditional relative frequency that it was preferred by a woman? Round to the nearest percent.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Comedy} & \text{Action} & \text{Drama} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Men} & 28 & 42 & 10 & 80 \\ \hline \text{Women} & 32 & 18 & 30 & 80 \\ \hline \text{Total} & 60 & 60 & 40 & 160 \\ \hline\end{array}$$
questions.explanation: Women who prefer Drama = 30. Total Drama = 40. 30/40 = 0.75 = 75%.

questions.question: A poll shows 55% of 400 randomly surveyed voters support Candidate A, with a margin of error of ±5%. What is the range of plausible support for Candidate A in the entire population?
questions.explanation: 55% ± 5% gives an interval of 50% to 60%. The true level of support is likely within this range.

questions.question:
The following stem-and-leaf plot shows ages at a family reunion. Key: 2|1 = 21 years old. What is the mode of the ages?

$$\begin{array}{c|c} \text{Stem} & \text{Leaf} \\ \hline 2 & 1\,3\,5\,8 \\ 3 & 0\,2\,4\,6\,6\,6\,9 \\ 4 & 1\,5\,5\,7 \\ 5 & 0\,2 \end{array}$$
questions.explanation: Data: 21, 23, 25, 28, 30, 32, 34, 36, 36, 36, 39, 41, 45, 45, 47, 50, 52. The value 36 appears three times, more than any other value.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can handle advanced data analysis including IQR, outliers, correlation, two-way tables, margins of error, and stem-and-leaf plots.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Review: IQR = Q3 - Q1. Outliers fall outside Q1 - 1.5×IQR and Q3 + 1.5×IQR. Correlation coefficient r near 1 or -1 indicates strong correlation.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: 1) Ordering data for median and quartiles, 2) Using the correct denominator for joint vs. conditional frequency, 3) Interpreting margin of error intervals.
scoreMessages.0: Start by: 1) Ordering data from least to greatest, 2) Finding median, Q1, Q3, and IQR, 3) Reading two-way tables for joint and conditional frequencies, 4) Using proportions with margins of error to find plausible ranges.
